principle.name: Consistent naming
principle.description: Use consistent naming conventions across all endpoints: kebab-case for paths, camelCase for JSON fields. Resource names should be plural nouns. Verb usage should be avoided in paths — use HTTP methods for actions.
principle.rationale: Consistency reduces cognitive load for consumers and prevents integration errors.

principle.name: Comprehensive error handling
principle.description: Every endpoint must define error responses for: validation failures (400), auth failures (401/403), not found (404), conflicts (409), rate limiting (429), and server errors (5xx). Use a consistent error response schema with code, message, and optional details.
principle.rationale: Clients need predictable error contracts to implement robust handling.

principle.name: Pagination on all list endpoints
principle.description: Every endpoint that returns a collection must support pagination. Define cursor-based or offset-based pagination with page size limits. Document default and max page sizes.
principle.rationale: Unbounded list responses cause performance and reliability issues at scale.

principle.name: Versioning strategy upfront
principle.description: Define the API versioning strategy before first release: URL path (/v1/...), header (Accept-Version), or query param. Document deprecation and sunset policy.
principle.rationale: Retrofitting versioning after release is costly and disruptive.

principle.name: Idempotency for writes
principle.description: All create and update operations that can be retried must support idempotency. Document idempotency key mechanism (header, request body field) and behavior for duplicate requests.
principle.rationale: Network retries and user double-clicks require idempotent handling to prevent duplicates.

principle.name: Auth on every endpoint
principle.description: Explicitly document auth requirements for every endpoint: public, authenticated, or role-based. No endpoint should have unspecified auth — default to authenticated unless explicitly public.
principle.rationale: Unspecified auth leads to security gaps and inconsistent enforcement.

pitfall.why_problematic: Confuses API consumers and makes discovery difficult.
Leads to unbounded responses, timeouts, and memory issues.
pitfall.correct_approach: Always define pagination parameters and enforce limits.

pitfall.why_problematic: Clients cannot implement proper error handling; debugging is difficult.
pitfall.correct_approach: Document all error codes and response schemas for each endpoint.

pitfall.why_problematic: Implementation may default incorrectly; security gaps emerge.
pitfall.correct_approach: Explicitly state auth for every endpoint in the spec.

pitfall.why_problematic: Breaking changes become painful; migration paths are unclear.
pitfall.correct_approach: Define versioning strategy and deprecation policy from day one.

category.name: before_writing_spec
category.item: All endpoints have HTTP method, path, and purpose documented.
Request/response schemas defined for each endpoint.
Error codes and schemas documented for all failure cases.
Pagination parameters defined for list endpoints.
